title: Confusion and Delirium
authors: S. Andrew Josephson, Bruce L. Miller

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.title: Recognizing Subtypes of Delirium: Hyperactive vs. Hypoactive
items.content: Delirium is characterized by an acute decline in cognition with fluctuating symptoms over hours or days, primarily marked by a deficit in attention. It manifests in two subtypes based on psychomotor features: hyperactive and hypoactive. The hyperactive subtype includes severe agitation, hallucinations, and autonomic instability, often seen in delirium tremens from alcohol withdrawal. Conversely, the hypoactive subtype is characterized by withdrawal, apathy, and psychomotor slowing, as seen with benzodiazepine intoxication.
keyPoints: Delirium involves a fluctuating acute decline in cognition over hours or days.
The hallmark of delirium is a deficit in attention.
Hyperactive subtype features severe agitation, hallucinations, and autonomic instability.
Hypoactive subtype includes withdrawal, apathy, and psychomotor slowing.
Patients may present with symptoms along the spectrum between hyperactive and hypoactive subtypes.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.title: The Importance of Recognizing and Treating Delirium to Prevent Long-term Consequences
items.content: Delirium is often underdiagnosed, particularly the hypoactive subtype, leading to significant morbidity and mortality. It can be reversible if promptly recognized and treated, as many causes like infections or medication effects are treatable. However, persistent delirium may result in permanent neuronal damage and long-term cognitive decline. Prevention strategies and early treatment of underlying etiologies are crucial to mitigate these risks.
keyPoints: Delirium is a major cause of morbidity and mortality with significant healthcare costs.
Recognition and treatment can reverse many causes of delirium, such as infections or medication effects.
Persistent delirium may lead to permanent neuronal damage and cognitive decline.
Prevention strategies are essential due to the potential for long-term consequences even after resolution.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.title: Identifying High-Risk Patients for Delirium Prevention
items.content: Delirium prevention strategies begin with identifying high-risk patients. Comprehensive programs screen all or most patients upon admission or before elective surgery, triggering focused prevention measures.
keyPoints: Older age (>65 years) and baseline cognitive dysfunction are major risk factors.
Patients aged >65 or with low cognition scores develop delirium at a rate of ~50% during hospitalization.
Other predisposing factors include sensory deprivation (e.g., hearing/visual impairment), immobility, malnutrition, and underlying medical/neurologic illness.
In-hospital risks: physical restraints, bladder catheterization, sleep/sensory deprivation, addition of three or more new medications.

items.title: The Interplay Between Delirium and Dementia
items.content: Distinguishing between delirium and dementia can be challenging due to significant overlap. Dementia is a major risk factor for delirium, with at least two-thirds of delirium cases occurring in patients with coexisting dementia.
keyPoints: Dementia and preexisting cognitive dysfunction are major risk factors for delirium.
At least two-thirds of delirium cases occur in patients with underlying dementia.
Dementia with Lewy bodies, characterized by fluctuating course, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and attentional deficit, increases vulnerability to delirium.
Delirium may herald the onset of a previously unrecognized brain disorder; post-acute episode screening for underlying conditions is recommended.

items.title: High Incidence and Serious Prognosis of Delirium in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Delirium is prevalent, particularly among hospitalized patients, with incidence rates ranging from 10% to over 50%. It is notably higher in elderly patients and those undergoing hip surgery. In ICU settings, delirium affects up to 75% of older patients. Delirium's under-recognition, especially in ICUs, underscores its role as a significant manifestation of organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Incidence ranges from 10% to >50% in hospitalized patients.
Rates approach 75% for older ICU patients.
In-hospital mortality rates among delirious patients range from 25% to 33%.
Delirium is associated with higher morbidity, longer hospital stays, and increased economic costs.

items.title: Pathogenesis of Delirium Involves Neurotransmitter Abnormalities and Brain Dysfunction
items.content: The pathogenesis of delirium involves diffuse cerebral dysfunction, with EEG often showing symmetric slowing. Key brain regions implicated include the brainstem, thalamus, prefrontal cortex, and parietal lobes. Neurotransmitter imbalances, particularly acetylcholine deficiency, play a crucial role, with anticholinergic medications frequently precipitating delirium.
keyPoints: EEG often reveals symmetric slowing indicative of diffuse cerebral dysfunction.
Acetylcholine deficiency is central to delirium pathogenesis.
Anticholinergic medications can trigger delirium.
Patients with preexisting dementia are more susceptible.

items.title: Delirium as a 'Stress Test for the Brain' in Predisposed Individuals
items.content: Not all individuals exposed to deliriogenic insults develop delirium, highlighting the role of predisposition. Delirium can unmask underlying neurologic conditions, acting as a stress test for the brain. This concept is crucial when previously healthy individuals develop delirium from minor insults, suggesting potential unrecognized neurological illnesses.
keyPoints: Delirium develops in predisposed individuals exposed to certain insults.
Healthy young adults may not be affected by low doses of anticholinergic medications, but elderly with dementia can develop delirium.
Delirium can unmask underlying neurologic conditions such as neurodegenerative diseases or multiple strokes.

items.title: Importance of Baseline Cognitive Function in Delirium Diagnosis
items.content: Understanding a patient's baseline cognitive function is crucial for diagnosing delirium, as it helps differentiate from chronic conditions like dementia.
keyPoints: Baseline cognitive function can be assessed via collateral sources or outpatient records.
Delirium represents an acute change from the cognitive baseline, usually developing over hours to days.
Without knowledge of baseline cognitive function, patients with dementia may be misdiagnosed as delirious.

items.title: Role of Medications in Delirium Etiology
items.content: Medications are a common cause of delirium, particularly those with anticholinergic or sedative properties.
keyPoints: Nearly one-third of all cases of delirium are secondary to medications, especially in the elderly.
Medication histories should include prescription, over-the-counter, and herbal substances.
Recent changes in dosing or formulation can contribute to delirium.

items.title: Establishing Time Course of Cognitive Change
items.content: Determining the time course of cognitive change is essential for diagnosing delirium and identifying treatable causes.
keyPoints: The onset of cognitive changes should be correlated with potential etiologies like medication changes or systemic infection.
An acute confusional state requires knowledge of when symptoms began to differentiate from chronic conditions.

items.title: Importance of Fluid Status Assessment in Delirious Patients
items.content: Assessing fluid status is crucial as both dehydration and fluid overload can be associated with delirium. These conditions are usually easily rectifiable, making early identification important for patient management.
keyPoints: Dehydration and fluid overload linked to delirium
Both conditions can often be corrected promptly

items.title: Assessing Attentional Deficits in Delirium
items.content: Attentional deficits are a hallmark of delirium. A simple bedside test like digit span forward can be used to assess attention, where healthy adults typically repeat five to seven digits. A digit span of four or less suggests an attentional deficit unless hearing or language barriers exist.
keyPoints: Attentional deficit is key in diagnosing delirium
Digit span test: Healthy adults repeat 5-7 digits
A digit span of ≤4 indicates possible attentional deficit

items.title: Medication-Induced Delirium: Recognizing Risk Factors and Timing
items.content: Delirium can be precipitated by various medications, especially those with anticholinergic properties, narcotics, and benzodiazepines. The risk is higher in elderly patients or those with baseline cognitive impairments, where even low doses may induce delirium. In contrast, less susceptible individuals require very high doses to experience similar effects.
keyPoints: Anticholinergic drugs, narcotics, and benzodiazepines are common offenders in medication-induced delirium.
Elderly patients with baseline dementia are at higher risk; low doses can trigger delirium.
Correlate timing of recent medication changes with onset of cognitive dysfunction.

items.title: Delirium in Younger Patients: Illicit Drugs and Toxins as Etiologies
items.content: In younger patients, delirium is frequently caused by illicit drugs and toxins. The rise of substances like fentanyl, synthetic cannabis, 'bath salts', MDMA, GHB, ketamine, and abused prescription drugs such as oral narcotics and benzodiazepines has increased the incidence of delirium in acute care settings.
keyPoints: Illicit drugs and toxins are common causes of delirium in younger patients.
Substances like fentanyl, synthetic cannabis, 'bath salts', MDMA, GHB, ketamine contribute to increased cases.
Prescription drug abuse (oral narcotics, benzodiazepines) is prevalent.

items.title: Metabolic and Systemic Infections as Etiologies of Delirium
items.content: Delirium can result from metabolic abnormalities such as electrolyte disturbances (sodium, calcium, magnesium, glucose), liver or renal failure, hypercarbia, hypoxemia, vitamin deficiencies (thiamine, B12), and endocrinopathies. Systemic infections, particularly in the elderly, are also common causes, with urinary tract infections being a frequent trigger.
keyPoints: Metabolic abnormalities like electrolyte disturbances can cause delirium.
Liver or renal failure, hypercarbia, hypoxemia, vitamin deficiencies (thiamine, B12), endocrinopathies are etiologies.
Systemic infections, especially urinary tract infections in the elderly, often lead to delirium.

items.title: Guiding Further Tests in Delirium with Initial Screening Labs
items.content: A cost-effective approach to managing delirium involves using history and physical examination to guide further tests after initial screening laboratory studies. Basic labs include a complete blood count, electrolyte panel, liver function tests, and renal function tests for all patients. In elderly patients, additional screenings for systemic infections are crucial.
keyPoints: Basic screening labs: Complete blood count, electrolyte panel, liver and renal function tests
Elderly patients: Include chest radiography, urinalysis and culture, possibly blood cultures
Younger individuals: Consider serum and urine drug and toxicology screening earlier
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.title: Imaging and Lumbar Puncture in Delirium Workup
items.content: Brain imaging is often unhelpful but may be pursued if initial workups are unrevealing. CT scans identify large masses and hemorrhages, while MRI provides detailed neuroanatomic information useful for identifying ischemic strokes and other conditions. If CNS infection is suspected post-imaging, a lumbar puncture should be performed immediately.
keyPoints: Brain imaging: Often unhelpful; proceed if workup is unrevealing
CT scan: Identifies large masses and hemorrhages
MRI: Best for acute ischemic strokes and neuroanatomic detail
Lumbar puncture (LP): Obtain after neuroimaging if CNS infection suspected
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.title: Addressing Underlying Causes of Delirium
items.content: Management of delirium should focus on treating the underlying inciting factors, such as infections or electrolyte disturbances, which often lead to prompt resolution. Pharmacological targeting of symptoms without addressing these causes can prolong confusion and obscure diagnostic information.
keyPoints: Treatment of systemic infections with appropriate antibiotics
Correction of electrolyte disturbances
Avoid pharmacological treatment of delirium symptoms alone
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.title: Implementing Non-Pharmacologic Interventions for Delirium
items.content: Non-pharmacologic interventions, such as reorientation techniques and environmental adjustments, are effective in reducing delirium. These include maintaining a consistent sleep-wake cycle, providing sensory aids like glasses or hearing aids, and engaging patients with activities.
keyPoints: Reorientation by nursing staff and family
Visible clocks, calendars, outside-facing windows to reduce confusion
Prevent sensory isolation with necessary aids (glasses/hearing aids)
Promote appropriate sleep-wake cycles; consider melatonin at night
Mimic home environment to treat/prevent delirium
Ensure access to home items like bedding and clothing
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.title: Optimizing Sleep Environment for Delirium Prevention
items.content: Creating a conducive sleep environment is crucial in preventing delirium. This involves minimizing disturbances by keeping the room dark and quiet, reducing caffeine intake, and using aids like eye masks or ear plugs. Additionally, grouping nighttime tasks to minimize patient disturbance and ensuring clear communication during necessary interactions can help maintain orientation.
keyPoints: Make room as dark and quiet as possible
Minimize caffeine intake
Use eye mask, ear plugs for sleep aid
Group nighttime tasks to reduce disturbances
Ensure glasses and hearing aids are on during night communication
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Judicious Use of Medications in Delirium Management
items.content: In managing delirium, physical restraints should be avoided due to their disorienting effects. Bed alarms and personal sitters are preferred for safety. Chemical restraints like antipsychotics should only be used as a last resort for severe agitation due to their ineffectiveness in preventing or treating delirium and associated increased mortality risks in the elderly. Benzodiazepines should be limited to cases of alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal.
keyPoints: Avoid physical restraints; use bed alarms/personal sitters
Antipsychotics ineffective for delirium prevention/treatment, increase mortality risk in elderly
Use antipsychotics only for severe agitation as a last resort
Limit benzodiazepines to alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal cases
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.question: Which cognitive domain is considered the hallmark of delirium?
items.explanation: The hallmark of delirium is a deficit of attention.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.question: Which subtype of delirium is characterized by severe agitation, tremor, hallucinations, and autonomic instability?
items.explanation: The hyperactive subtype features prominent hallucinations, agitation, and autonomic instability.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.question: What is a common reason hypoactive delirium patients are underdiagnosed?
items.explanation: Patients who are quietly hypoactive are more often overlooked and underdiagnosed.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.question: Which two risk factors are most consistently identified for the development of delirium?
options: Older age and baseline cognitive dysfunction
Sensory deprivation and use of physical restraints
Inadequate pain treatment and inhalational anesthetics
items.explanation: Older age and baseline cognitive dysfunction are the two most consistently identified risk factors for delirium.

items.question: What is a common in-hospital risk factor for delirium?
items.explanation: The use of physical restraints is a common in-hospital risk factor for delirium.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by fluctuating course, prominent visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism?
items.explanation: Dementia with Lewy bodies is characterized by a fluctuating course, prominent visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.

items.question: What is the estimated range of in-hospital mortality rates among patients with delirium?
items.explanation: In-hospital mortality rates for delirious patients range from 25% to 33%, similar to those due to sepsis.

items.question: Which brain regions are most commonly associated with the attentional deficits seen in delirium?
options: Brainstem, thalamus, prefrontal cortex, and parietal lobes
items.explanation: The attentional deficit in delirium has a diffuse localization within the brainstem, thalamus, prefrontal cortex, and parietal lobes.

items.question: Which neurotransmitter deficiency is considered to play a key role in delirium pathogenesis?
items.explanation: Deficiency of acetylcholine may play a key role in the pathogenesis of delirium.

items.question: In which population is delirium most commonly underrecognized?
items.explanation: Delirium is not recognized in up to one-third of elderly inpatients, and diagnosis is especially problematic in the ICU.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a validated instrument used to assess baseline cognitive function via a collateral source?
items.explanation: The Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) is used for diagnosing delirium, not specifically assessing baseline cognitive function.

items.question: What percentage of all cases of delirium are estimated to be secondary to medications?
items.explanation: Nearly one-third (approximately 33%) of all cases of delirium are estimated to be secondary to medications.

items.question: What is the typical time course for the development of delirium?
items.explanation: Delirium usually develops over hours to days, representing a change from baseline cognitive function.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically used as a collateral source for history in delirious patients?
items.explanation: A patient's employer is not typically used as a collateral source for obtaining an accurate history.

items.question: Which physical sign can be indicative of hepatic encephalopathy in a patient with delirium?
items.explanation: The appearance of the skin showing jaundice is associated with hepatic encephalopathy.

items.question: What can a digit span forward test help assess in patients suspected of having delirium?
items.explanation: A digit span forward test is used to screen for an attentional deficit, a hallmark of delirium.

items.question: Why might the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) be unreliable in patients with delirium?
options: Attentional deficits impair performance on tasks
It does not assess motor function
items.explanation: Delirious patients' attentional deficits render the MMSE unreliable as it impairs their ability to perform certain tasks.

items.question: Which group of medications is particularly noted for precipitating delirium due to their properties?
items.explanation: Drugs with anticholinergic properties, narcotics, and benzodiazepines are particularly common offenders in precipitating delirium.

items.question: What is a common cause of hyperactive delirium related to substance use?
items.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal, rather than high serum levels from alcohol abuse, is a common cause of hyperactive delirium.

items.question: Which metabolic abnormality can lead to substantial cognitive disturbances in susceptible individuals?
items.explanation: Electrolyte disturbances such as sodium, calcium, magnesium, or glucose can cause delirium.

items.question: What is a common scenario involving systemic infections that lead to delirium in elderly patients?
options: Development of acute cognitive decline with urinary tract infection
items.explanation: An acute cognitive decline in the setting of a urinary tract infection is common in elderly patients with baseline dementia.

items.question: Which initial laboratory tests should be obtained in all patients with delirium?
options: Complete blood count, electrolyte panel, and liver and renal function tests
Serum and urine drug and toxicology screening
items.explanation: Basic screening lab tests for all delirium patients include a complete blood count, electrolyte panel, and liver and renal function tests.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.question: In which scenario is brain imaging often unhelpful in patients with delirium?
options: When an offending medication is identified as the precipitant
When initial workup reveals a clear etiology
During the initial evaluation of all patients
items.explanation: Multiple studies have shown that brain imaging in delirium patients is often unhelpful during the initial evaluation.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.question: What should be done if neuroimaging does not reveal a cause for delirium and CNS infection is suspected?
options: Obtain serum and urine drug screening
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture must be obtained immediately after neuroimaging if CNS infection is suspected.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.question: Which of the following interventions is NOT recommended in the management of delirium?
options: Blindly targeting symptoms with pharmacological agents.
Reorientation by nursing staff and family.
Providing glasses and hearing aids to patients who need them.
Maintaining proper nutrition and volume status.
items.explanation: Pharmacologically targeting delirium symptoms without addressing the underlying cause can prolong confusion.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.question: Which intervention is crucial for reducing sensory isolation in patients with delirium?
options: Ensuring the patient has access to glasses and hearing aids if needed.
items.explanation: Providing glasses and hearing aids helps prevent sensory isolation, which can reduce confusion in patients.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.question: What is the primary reason for maintaining proper sleep-wake cycles in delirium management?
options: To ensure patients receive adequate nutrition.
To prevent napping during the day and promote restful sleep at night.
To reduce the need for pharmacological intervention.
To allow family members to visit more frequently.
items.explanation: Maintaining proper sleep-wake cycles helps prevent napping during the day and promotes restful sleep, reducing delirium.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.question: Which intervention is recommended to minimize patient disorientation during nighttime care?
options: Ensure glasses and hearing aids are on if communicating with the patient at night.
Administer very-low-dose antipsychotic medications routinely overnight.
Use physical restraints to prevent patient movement.
items.explanation: Ensuring glasses and hearing aids are on helps minimize disorientation during nighttime care by facilitating communication.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended approach for managing patients who pose a threat to themselves or staff?
options: Use physical restraints as the first line of management.
Implement bed alarms and personal sitters when necessary.
Prescribe benzodiazepines for all agitated patients.
items.explanation: Bed alarms and personal sitters are preferred as they are less disorienting than physical restraints.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which medication class should be avoided in treating delirium due to its potential to worsen confusion?
items.explanation: Benzodiazepines can worsen confusion through their sedative properties and should be avoided unless delirium is due to alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.statement: Delirium is always a permanent condition.
items.explanation: Delirium is potentially reversible, although some episodes may persist or cause long-term cognitive decline.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.subtopic: Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM
items.statement: Hyperactive delirium patients are often easily recognized due to their characteristic severe agitation.
items.explanation: Hyperactive patients with delirium exhibit severe agitation, making them more recognizable.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Confusion and Delirium | CLINICAL FEATURES OF DELIRIUM

items.statement: At least two-thirds of cases of delirium occur in patients without any underlying dementia.
items.explanation: At least two-thirds of cases of delirium occur in patients with coexisting underlying dementia.

items.statement: Avoiding the use of physical restraints can help prevent delirium.
items.explanation: Avoiding risks such as the use of physical restraints is a key component of delirium prevention.

items.statement: Delirium was previously considered a transient condition with a benign prognosis.
items.explanation: An episode of delirium was once viewed as a transient condition with a benign prognosis.

items.statement: Delirium is primarily caused by focal lesions such as ischemic strokes.
items.explanation: While rare, delirium can result from focal lesions; however, it most commonly results from widespread disturbances in cortical and subcortical regions.

items.statement: Establishing the time course of cognitive change is crucial only for diagnosing delirium, not for identifying treatable etiologies.
items.explanation: The time course helps correlate onset with potentially treatable causes like medication changes or infections.

items.statement: A history of illicit drug use, alcoholism, or toxin exposure is important in the evaluation of delirious patients.
items.explanation: This information is particularly relevant for younger delirious patients.

items.statement: Focal strokes or mass lesions are common isolated causes of delirium.
items.explanation: Focal strokes or mass lesions in isolation rarely cause delirium.

items.statement: Assessment for attentional deficits is crucial even if the patient's level of consciousness appears normal.
items.explanation: A screen for an attentional deficit is necessary, as it is a classic hallmark of delirium.

items.statement: Delirium can occur even with low doses of medication in elderly patients with baseline dementia.
items.explanation: Elderly patients with baseline dementia may become delirious upon exposure to relatively low doses of a medication.

items.statement: CNS infections are the most common etiologies of delirium.
items.explanation: CNS infections such as meningitis, encephalitis, and abscess are less common causes of delirium.

items.statement: MRI is the preferred initial imaging modality for all patients with delirium.
items.explanation: CT scanning is often used initially due to its availability and speed, while MRI is considered if etiology remains elusive.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.statement: EEG should be considered in delirious patients when no cause is readily identified.
items.explanation: EEG is invaluable if seizures are suspected or if there's no clear cause for the delirium.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.statement: Blindly targeting symptoms of delirium pharmacologically often leads to prompt resolution.
items.explanation: Targeting symptoms without addressing the underlying cause can prolong confusion.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.statement: Simple standard nursing practices like maintaining proper nutrition and volume status help alleviate delirium.
items.explanation: Proper nutrition and hydration are essential in reducing discomfort and confusion.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care

items.statement: Chemical restraints are the first-line treatment for patients with severe agitation.
items.explanation: Chemical restraints should be avoided and reserved only for cases where there is a significant risk of harm.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Melatonin has been definitively proven effective in preventing delirium.
items.explanation: While melatonin shows promise, it has not been definitively proven to be effective for delirium prevention.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · PROMOTE SLEEP | B | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Delirium should be viewed as an important manifestation of organ dysfunction.
items.reason: The condition is often unrecognized and represents a significant underlying illness, similar to liver or kidney failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; delirium is indeed akin to other forms of organ dysfunction.

items.assertion: Patients with Alzheimer's disease are less susceptible to episodes of delirium.
items.reason: Alzheimer's disease is associated with cholinergic deficiency, which plays a role in the pathogenesis of delirium.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients with Alzheimer's disease are particularly susceptible to delirium due to cholinergic deficiency.

items.assertion: A diagnosis of delirium can be made without knowing the patient's baseline cognitive function.
items.reason: Baseline cognitive function is crucial for distinguishing delirium from other conditions like dementia or depression.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because knowledge of baseline cognitive function is essential to diagnose delirium accurately.

items.assertion: Medications with anticholinergic or sedative properties are common causes of delirium.
items.reason: Nearly one-third of all cases of delirium are secondary to medications, especially in the elderly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these medications cause delirium.

items.assertion: Intermittent seizures may be overlooked when considering etiologies of delirium.
items.reason: A fluctuating course is often seen in delirium, which can mask intermittent seizure activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why intermittent seizures may be overlooked.

items.assertion: Delirium at the end of life in palliative care settings is uncommon.
items.reason: Patients often experience delirium due to systemic infection as a common etiology.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it is extremely common for patients to experience delirium at the end of life.

items.assertion: Mimicking the home environment can help treat and prevent delirium.
items.reason: It makes the hospital environment less foreign, reducing confusion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Confusion and Delirium · Delirium | Delirium Reduction Care
